Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study when I felt like it or when something was due soon, which wasn't super organized. Most of my studying happened in random bursts, like late at night when I finally got around to it. I'd spend maybe 29 hours a week total, but it was scattered all over the place. I just kind of drifted through it without a real plan.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place depending on what was coming up. If there was a deadline, I'd suddenly kick into gear and study more intensely. Otherwise, I'd let days go by without really touching anything. I didn't have a set schedule like some people do with specific study times. It just depended on my mood and what else was going on in my life.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I absolutely had to, like when an assignment was due or when I needed to check something specific. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if I'd missed anything important since I don't attend class that much. It wasn't really a habit for me, more like a necessary thing I did when I couldn't avoid it anymore. I'd scroll through quickly and then log off.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became part of my normal routine because I just didn't feel drawn to it. I'd only go on when I needed something specific, and then I'd leave. It felt more like a chore than something that fit naturally into my day. I guess I never found a reason to make it a regular thing.
Q5: The reading materials were probably the most useful for me since I learn better by reading and writing things down. I'd download PDFs and go through them on my own time without needing to be online. The lecture notes were decent too when I actually looked at them. Videos were okay but I didn't watch them as much as I probably should have.
Q6: I basically ignored the discussion forums completely. They felt pointless to me, like I didn't see how posting there would help me learn anything. I also skipped a lot of the optional stuff because I just couldn't be bothered. If it wasn't required, it wasn't happening. I had other things going on and limited energy to spend on extras.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it was directly connected to an assignment or exam. If I could see how it would help my grade, I'd do it. But if it was just supplementary or for enrichment, I'd usually skip it. I needed a clear reason to spend my energy on something. Otherwise it just felt like busywork.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely different. Required activities had this weight to them, like I knew I had to do them eventually. Optional ones just faded into the background because there was no consequence for skipping them. I rarely did anything optional unless I was extremely bored, which wasn't often. The pressure wasn't there so neither was my motivation.
Q9: Definitely. I downloaded a lot of the reading materials and studied from those offline since I don't always have reliable internet access. I'd print things out or save them to my phone and go through them later. I also used my own notes a lot, writing things down by hand to help me remember. A lot of my studying happened away from the computer entirely.
Q10: Most of my actual learning happened with printed notes and downloaded files spread out on my bed. I'd spend hours going over stuff but none of that would show up online. Sometimes I'd read the same PDF multiple times offline but the LMS would only show I downloaded it once. My study time was real, it just wasn't visible in any system.
Q11: All the time. I'd download something once and then work with it offline for days. It was easier that way since I didn't have to worry about internet connection. I'd annotate the files, make notes, and study from them repeatedly. The LMS would just show one download but I'd have used that material way more than once.
Q12: There were definitely stretches where I just couldn't bring myself to care. I'd feel bored and disconnected from everything, like the course content just didn't matter to me. Sometimes I was dealing with stuff at home or just felt mentally checked out. During those times, logging in felt like too much effort. I'd let things pile up and deal with it later.
Q13: Yeah, when I had other responsibilities piling up, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd feel tired or overwhelmed and just not have the energy to engage properly. Sometimes I was confused about what I was supposed to be doing, which made me avoid it even more. It was easier to just not deal with it until I absolutely had to.
Q14: Plenty of times. I'd be scrolling through materials but my brain was somewhere else entirely. I'd watch a video without really absorbing anything or skim through readings without processing them. It was like going through the motions without actually learning. My body was there but my mind had checked out.
Q15: I postponed things constantly even when I knew they mattered. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow and then tomorrow would come and I'd say the same thing. Part of it was just not feeling motivated enough to start. Starting always felt like the hardest part. I'd wait until the pressure got intense enough to force me into action.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I'd just drift along doing nothing productive. When a deadline was close, I'd suddenly log in more and actually do the work. It was stressful but it was the only system that worked for me. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. I'd read it, think about it for a minute, and then move on. It didn't make me more engaged or motivated to do better next time. Maybe if the feedback had been more personal or encouraging it would've mattered more. But it just felt like another thing to check off and forget about.
Q18: The structure was okay but it didn't really push me to engage more. Having 13 online courses meant everything kind of blurred together. I couldn't keep track of what was happening in each one. A more rigid structure with clearer expectations might have helped me stay on track. But I also probably would've ignored that too.
Q19: The lack of interactive elements made it easier to disengage. Everything felt very passive, like I was just supposed to read and absorb without doing much. If there had been more hands-on activities or things that required active participation, maybe I would've paid more attention. But the design made it easy to zone out and coast through.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for things or trying to figure out what's going on without actually completing work. They could be confused or overwhelmed and spending time clicking around without making progress. Or maybe they're just procrastinating online, looking busy without being productive. Activity doesn't always mean accomplishment.
Q21: That definitely sounds like me. I didn't log in much but I still managed to do okay on exams and assignments. I studied offline a lot and focused on what actually mattered for my grades. You don't need to be constantly online to learn the material. Sometimes less online activity just means you're efficient, not disengaged.
Q22: A course log can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but it can't show what's happening in your head. It misses all the offline studying, the thinking you do while walking around, the notes you write by hand. It also can't tell if you're actually focused or just scrolling mindlessly. It's a very incomplete picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in real effort. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials. It's about being mentally present and wanting to understand the content. For me, engagement was rare because I mostly felt bored and disconnected. But when I did engage, it was usually right before something was due.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied offline with printed materials and handwritten notes. It wouldn't show that I actually understood the content well enough to score high on exams despite low attendance. It also wouldn't show my general sense of boredom and disconnection from the whole process. Numbers don't capture how I actually felt about the course.
